sentences.base_language: On weekends I like to relax and enjoy my free time.
sentences.target_language: في عطلة نهاية الأسبوع أحب أن أسترخي وأستمتع بوقت فراغي.
sentences.romanized: fi 'utlat nihayat al-usbu' uhibb an astarkhi wa-astamti' bi-waqt faraghi.
sentences.literal: In weekend I-like that I-relax and-I-enjoy with-time my-free.
sentences.grammarNote: عُطلَة نِهَايَة الأُسبُوع (ʿuṭlat nihāyat al-usbūʿ, weekend) — a double iḍāfa chain: عُطلَة (holiday, from root ع-ط-ل, to be idle) + نِهَايَة (end, from ن-ه-ي) + الأُسبُوع (the week). أُحِبّ أَن أَسترخِي (uḥibb an astarkhi, I like to relax) — Form X of ر-خ-و (r-kh-w, to be soft/relaxed): اِسترخَى. وَقت فَرَاغِي (waqt farāghī, my free time) — iḍāfa with pronoun: وَقت (time) + فَرَاغِي (my free time, فَرَاغ from root ف-ر-غ, to be empty).

sentences.base_language: I usually start my day with reading.
sentences.grammarNote: عادةً ('adatan) = usually. Adverbs of frequency like عادةً, دائماً (always), and أحياناً (sometimes) come at the start of the sentence in Arabic, before the verb.

sentences.base_language: I love reading novels and history books.
sentences.grammarNote: قِرَاءَة (qirāʾa, reading) — verbal noun Form I of ق-ر-أ (q-r-ʾ, to read): قَرَأَ (to read), قِرَاءَة (reading), قُرآن (the Quran — 'the reading/recitation'). الرِّوَايَات (ar-riwāyāt, novels) — sound feminine plural of رِوَايَة (novel/narration, Form IV verbal noun of ر-و-ي, to narrate). كُتُب التَّارِيخ (kutub at-tārīkh, history books) — iḍāfa: كُتُب (books, broken plural of كِتَاب, pattern فُعُل) + التارِيخ.

sentences.base_language: In the afternoon I go for a walk in the park.
sentences.target_language: في فترة بعد الظهر أذهب للمشي في الحديقة.
sentences.romanized: fi fatrat ba'd adh-dhuhr adhhab lil-mashy fi al-hadiqa.
sentences.literal: In period after the-noon I-go for-the-walking in the-park.
sentences.grammarNote: فَترَة بَعدَ الظُّهر (fatrat baʿd aẓ-ẓuhr, the afternoon period) — فَترَة (period/span, from root ف-ت-ر, to slacken) + بَعدَ الظُّهر (after noon). لِلمَشي (lil-mashī, for walking) — لِـ + المَشي: verbal noun of مَشَى (to walk, Form I of م-ش-ي). الحَدِيقَة (al-ḥadīqa, the park/garden) from root ح-د-ق (to encircle): an enclosed green space.

sentences.base_language: The park near my house is very beautiful.
sentences.target_language: الحديقة بالقرب من بيتي جميلة جداً.
sentences.romanized: al-hadiqa bil-qurb min bayti jamila jiddan.
sentences.literal: The-park by-the-nearness from my-house beautiful very.
sentences.grammarNote: بِالقُرب مِن (bil-qurb min, near/close to) — بِـ + القُرب (the nearness, verbal noun of ق-ر-ب, to be near) + مِن: a fixed prepositional phrase of location. بَيتِي (baytī, my house) — بَيت (house, from root ب-ي-ت) + suffix ي (my). جَمِيلَة (jamīla, beautiful, feminine) agrees with الحَدِيقَة (feminine). جِدَّاً (jiddan, very) is an invariable intensifier.

sentences.base_language: Sometimes I meet friends there and we walk together.
sentences.target_language: أحياناً أقابل أصدقاء هناك ونمشي معاً.
sentences.romanized: ahyanan uqabil asdiqa' hunak wa-namshi ma'an.
sentences.literal: Sometimes I-meet friends there and-we-walk together.
sentences.grammarNote: أَحيَاناً (aḥyānan, sometimes) — accusative adverb, broken plural of حِين (time/while): أَحيَان (times) + tanwin ـاً. أُقَابِل (uqābil, I meet) — Form III of ق-ب-ل (q-b-l, to face/receive): قَابَلَ = to meet face-to-face. أَصدِقَاء (aṣdiqāʾ, friends) — broken plural of صَدِيق (friend), pattern أَفعِلَاء. مَعاً (maʿan, together) — adverb from مَع (with) + tanwin accusative.

sentences.base_language: In the evening I watch football on TV.
sentences.target_language: في المساء أشاهد كرة القدم على التلفاز.
sentences.romanized: fi al-masa' ushahid kurat al-qadam 'ala at-tilfaz.
sentences.literal: In the-evening I-watch ball the-foot on the-television.
sentences.grammarNote: أُشَاهِد (ushāhid, I watch) — Form III of ش-ه-د (sh-h-d, to witness/watch): شَاهَدَ = to watch/observe. كُرَة القَدَم (kurat al-qadam, football) — iḍāfa: كُرَة (ball, from root ك-ر-و/ر) + القَدَم (the foot). This is the standard Arabic term for football/soccer: literally 'ball of the foot.' التِّلفَاز (at-tilfāz, television) — an Arabicised loanword; also تَلِيفِزيُون.
Possession in Arabic is shown by attaching a suffix to the noun: فريق (fariq = team) + ي (i = my) → فريقي (my team). No word for 'of' is needed; the suffix does the job.

sentences.base_language: They play really well this season.
sentences.grammarNote: يَلعَبُون (yal'abūn, they play) — Form I masculine plural present of ل-ع-ب (l-ʿ-b, to play): the ـُون (-ūn) suffix marks the masculine plural. بِشَكل رَائِع (bi-shakl rāʾiʿ, in a wonderful manner/really well) — the بِـ + noun pattern for adverbs: بِشَكل + adjective is extremely common in MSA. هَذَا المَوسِم (hādhā al-mawsim, this season) — demonstrative + definite noun.

sentences.base_language: I am happy when they win.
sentences.grammarNote: عِندَمَا (ʿindamā, when) — a temporal conjunction: عِند (at the time of) + مَا. يَفُوزُون (yafūzūn, they win) — Form I of ف-و-ز (f-w-z, to win/succeed): فَازَ (he won), فَوز (victory). أَنَا سَعِيد (ana saʿīd, I am happy) — a nominal sentence: pronoun + adjective, no verb needed in the present tense. سَعِيد from root س-ع-د (to be fortunate/happy): سَعَادَة (happiness), سَعِيد (happy — also a common name).

sentences.target_language: عطلة نهاية الأسبوع تمر بسرعة كبيرة!
sentences.grammarNote: بِسُرعة (bi-sur'a) = quickly / fast. The prefix بـ (bi-) + a noun creates an adverbial phrase: بِسُرعة (quickly), بِسُهولة (easily), بِهُدوء (calmly). This pattern is very common in Arabic.
